items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.misconception: Six beats per bar, all equal in stress.
items.why: Students count 6/8 as six equal beats. In practice it's felt as TWO dotted-crotchet beats subdivided into three quavers each - compound duple, not simple sextuple.

items.q: What three notes form a major triad in root position?
items.why: Students think 'three notes = a triad'. Triads are specific: stacked thirds from the root. C major = C, E, G (skipping D and F). Consecutive scale notes are clusters, not triads.

items.q: What is characteristic of Baroque music?
items.correct: Continuous bass line, ornamentation, terraced dynamics, and counterpoint.
items.why: Students project later styles backwards. Baroque (1600-1750) used basso continuo, harpsichord/organ, and terraced (sudden) dynamics. Piano hadn't yet replaced harpsichord.
It's Schoenberg. “Stravinsky” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which composer is most associated with minimalist works such as 'Music for 18 Musicians'?
items.why: It's Reich. “Glass” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did John Cage compose his silent work '4'33"'?
items.why: Exact answer: 1952. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
